Prior to the war, during the [Asturian miners' strike of 1934](/wiki/Asturian_miners%27_strike_of_1934 "Asturian miners' strike of 1934"), religious buildings were burnt and at least 100 clergy, religious civilians, and pro-Catholic police were killed by revolutionaries.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1973-188)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoverdale2002148-198) Franco had brought in Spain's [colonial Army of Africa](/wiki/Army_of_Africa_(Spain) "Army of Africa (Spain)") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Ejército de África* or *Cuerpo de Ejército Marroquí*) and reduced the miners to submission by heavy artillery attacks and bombing raids. The [Spanish Legion](/wiki/Spanish_Legion "Spanish Legion") committed atrocities and the army carried out summary executions of leftists. The repression in the aftermath was brutal and prisoners were tortured.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200679-199) The Moroccan *[Fuerzas Regulares Indígenas](/wiki/Regulares "Regulares")* joined the rebellion and played a significant role in the civil war.[[194]](#cite_note-200)

While the Nationalists are often assumed to have drawn in the majority of military officers, this is a somewhat simplistic analysis. The Spanish army had its own internal divisions and long-standing rifts. Officers supporting the coup tended to be *africanistas* (men who fought in North Africa between 1909 and 1923) while those who stayed loyal tended to be *peninsulares* (men who stayed back in Spain during this period). This was because during Spain's North African campaigns, the traditional promotion by seniority was suspended in favour of promotion by merit through battlefield heroism. This tended to benefit younger officers starting their careers as they could, while older officers had familial commitments that made it harder for them to be deployed in North Africa. Officers in front line combat corps (primarily infantry and cavalry) benefited over those in technical corps (those in artillery, engineering etc.) because they had more chances to demonstrate the requisite battlefield heroism and had also traditionally enjoyed promotion by seniority. The *peninsulares* resented seeing the *africanistas* rapidly leapfrog through the ranks, while the *africanistas* themselves were seen as swaggering and arrogant, further fueling resentment. Thus, when the coup occurred, officers who joined the rebellion, particularly from Franco's rank downwards, were often *africanistas*, while senior officers and those in non-front line positions tended to oppose it (though a small number of senior *africanistas* opposed the coup as well).[[112]](#cite_note-Esdaile,_Charles_J_2018-118) It has also been argued that officers who stayed loyal to the Republic were more likely to have been promoted and to have been favoured by the Republican regime (such as those in the Aviation and Assault Guard units).[[195]](#cite_note-201) Thus, while often thought of as a "rebellion of the generals", this is not correct. Of the eighteen division generals, only four rebelled (of the four division generals without postings, two rebelled and two remained loyal). Fourteen of the fifty-six brigade generals rebelled. The rebels tended to draw from less senior officers. Of the approximately 15,301 officers, just over half rebelled.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova20109157-202)

### Other factions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other factions")]

Catalan and Basque nationalists were divided. Left-wing [Catalan nationalists](/wiki/Catalan_nationalists "Catalan nationalists") sided with the Republicans, while Conservative Catalan nationalists were far less vocal in supporting the government, due to anti-[clericalism](/wiki/Clericalism "Clericalism") and [confiscations](/wiki/Confiscation "Confiscation") occurring in areas within its control. [Basque nationalists](/wiki/Basque_nationalists "Basque nationalists"), heralded by the conservative [Basque Nationalist Party](/wiki/Basque_Nationalist_Party "Basque Nationalist Party"), were mildly supportive of the Republican government, although some in Navarre sided with the uprising for the same reasons influencing conservative Catalans. Notwithstanding religious matters, Basque nationalists, who were for the most part Catholic, generally sided with the Republicans, although the PNV, Basque nationalist party, was reported passing the plans of Bilbao defences to the Nationalists, in an attempt to reduce the duration and casualties of siege.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeersHogan1936529–544-203)

## Foreign involvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Foreign involvement")]

Main articles: [Foreign involvement in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Foreign_involvement_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Foreign involvement in the Spanish Civil War"), [International relations (1919–1939)](/wiki/International_relations_(1919%E2%80%931939) "International relations (1919–1939)"), and [Non-intervention in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Non-intervention_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Non-intervention in the Spanish Civil War")

The Spanish Civil War exposed political divisions across Europe. The right and the Catholics supported the Nationalists to stop the spread of [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism"). On the left, including labour unions, students and intellectuals, the war represented a necessary battle to stop the spread of fascism. Anti-war and pacifist sentiment was strong in many countries, leading to warnings that the Civil War could escalate into a second world war.[[198]](#cite_note-204) In this respect, the war was an indicator of the growing instability across Europe.[[199]](#cite_note-205) The 1930s saw Spain become a focus for [pacifist](/wiki/Pacifist "Pacifist") organisations, including the [Fellowship of Reconciliation](/wiki/Fellowship_of_Reconciliation "Fellowship of Reconciliation"), the [War Resisters League](/wiki/War_Resisters_League "War Resisters League"), and the [War Resisters' International](/wiki/War_Resisters%27_International "War Resisters' International"). Many people including, as they are now called, the *insumisos* ("defiant ones", [conscientious objectors](/wiki/Conscientious_objector "Conscientious objector")) argued and worked for non-violent strategies.[[note 3]](#cite_note-206)

The Spanish Civil War involved large numbers of non-Spanish citizens who participated in combat and advisory positions. [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [France](/wiki/Third_French_Republic "Third French Republic") led a [political alliance](/wiki/Political_alliance "Political alliance") of 27 nations that pledged [non-intervention](/wiki/Non-intervention_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Non-intervention in the Spanish Civil War"), including an embargo on all arms exports to Spain. The United States unofficially adopted a position of non-intervention as well, despite abstaining from joining the alliance (due in part to its policy of [political isolation](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism")). The group from the United States called themselves the "Abraham Lincoln Brigade". Germany, Italy and the Soviet Union signed on officially, but ignored the embargo. The attempted suppression of imported material was largely ineffective, and France was especially accused of allowing large shipments to Republican troops.[[200]](#cite_note-207) The clandestine actions of the various European powers were, at the time, considered to be risking another world war, alarming antiwar elements across the world.[[201]](#cite_note-208)

The [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")' reaction to the war was influenced by a fear of communism,[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson1974194-209) and was insufficient to contain the massive importation of arms and other war resources by the fighting factions. Although a Non-Intervention Committee was formed, its policies accomplished very little and its directives were ineffective.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStoff2004194-210)

### Support for the Nationalists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Support for the Nationalists")]

Main articles: [Italian military intervention in Spain](/wiki/Italian_military_intervention_in_Spain "Italian military intervention in Spain") and [German involvement in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/German_involvement_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "German involvement in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2006-1204-510%2C_Spanien%2C_Schlacht_um_Guadalajara.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2006-1204-510%2C_Spanien%2C_Schlacht_um_Guadalajara.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2006-1204-510,_Spanien,_Schlacht_um_Guadalajara.jpg)

[Italian troops](/wiki/Corpo_Truppe_Volontarie "Corpo Truppe Volontarie") manning a [10 cm howitzer](/wiki/10_cm_M._14_Feldhaubitze "10 cm M. 14 Feldhaubitze") at [Guadalajara](/wiki/Guadalajara,_Castile-La_Mancha "Guadalajara, Castile-La Mancha"), 1937

[Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") joined the war to secure Fascist control of the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"), as the conquest of Ethiopia in the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") made the Italian government confident in its military power.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006135–136-211) Italy became the stronger backer of the Nationalists.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeulen200025-212) Italy supplied machine guns, artillery, aircraft, [tankettes](/wiki/Tankette "Tankette"), the [Aviazione Legionaria](/wiki/Aviazione_Legionaria "Aviazione Legionaria"), and the [Corpo Truppe Volontarie](/wiki/Corpo_Truppe_Volontarie "Corpo Truppe Volontarie") (CTV) to the Nationalist cause, and assisted with the Mediterranean blockade.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006199-213) The Italian CTV would supply the Nationalists with 50,000 men.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006199-213) Italian warships took part in breaking the Republican navy's [blockade](/wiki/Blockade "Blockade") of Nationalist-held Spanish Morocco and took part in naval bombardment of Republican-held Málaga, Valencia, and Barcelona.[[207]](#cite_note-214) Italian air raids targeted mainly cities and civilians.[[208]](#cite_note-:1-215) These Italian commitments were heavily propagandised in Italy and became a point of fascist pride.[[208]](#cite_note-:1-215)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E20569-21%2C_Spanien%2C_Ausbildung_durch_%22Legion_Condor%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E20569-21%2C_Spanien%2C_Ausbildung_durch_%22Legion_Condor%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-E20569-21,_Spanien,_Ausbildung_durch_%22Legion_Condor%22.jpg)

German officer from the Condor Legion instructing Nationalist infantry soldiers, Ávila

Despite the German signing of a non-intervention agreement in September 1936, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") gave various aid and military support for the Nationalists,[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961259–261-216) including the formation of the [Condor Legion](/wiki/Condor_Legion "Condor Legion") as a land and air force.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200487-217) Germany successfully flew the [Army of Africa](/wiki/Army_of_Africa_(Spain) "Army of Africa (Spain)") to [Mainland Spain](/wiki/Mainland_Spain "Mainland Spain") in the early stages of the war.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200416-218) The [bombing of Guernica](/wiki/Bombing_of_Guernica "Bombing of Guernica"), on 26 April 1937, would be the most controversial event of German involvement, with perhaps 200 to 300 civilians killed.[[212]](#cite_note-219) German involvement also included Operation Ursula, a [U-boat](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") undertaking and contributions from the [Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine").[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200415-220) Strategically, Nazi support for Franco provided a distraction from Hitler's [central European strategy](/wiki/Nazi_Germany#Military_build-up "Nazi Germany") and created a friendly Spanish state to threaten France.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200416-218)

Germany's Condor Legion spearheaded many Nationalist victories, particularly in the air dominance from 1937 onward.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200488-221) Spain was a proving ground for German tank and aircraft tactics, the latter being only moderately successful.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200488–89-222) Germany trained 56,000 Nationalist soldiers, who were technically proficient and covered infantry, tanks and anti-tank units; air and anti-aircraft forces; and those trained in naval warfare.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200487-217) About 16,000 German citizens fought in the conflict, mostly as pilots, ground crew, artillery and tank crew and military advisers and instructors. About 10,000 Germans were in Spain at the peak.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961634-223) German aid to the Nationalists amounted to approximately £43,000,000 ($215,000,000) in 1939 prices, mostly for the Condor Legion.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961634-223) No detailed list of German supplies furnished to Spain has been found.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961634-223)

Portugal supplied the Nationalists with critical ammunition and logistical resources.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006116,_133,_143,_148,_174,_427-224) Throughout the war, Portugal ensured that Iberian borders would continue to supply the Nationalists. The Nationalists even referred to Lisbon as "the port of Castile".[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006116,_198-225) Portuguese Prime Minister [António de Oliveira Salazar](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar") semi-officially endorsed the "[Viriatos](/wiki/Viriatos "Viriatos")" volunteer force of 8,000[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOthen200879-226) to 20,000.[[220]](#cite_note-227) With Franco's victory increasingly certain, Portugal recognised Franco's regime and soon after the war signed a treaty of friendship and non-aggression pact, the [Iberian Pact](/wiki/Iberian_Pact "Iberian Pact").[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagher198386-228) Portugal played an important diplomatic role in supporting Franco and insisting to the British government that Franco sought to replicate Salazar's *[Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)")*, not Mussolini's Fascist Italy or Hitler's Nazi Germany.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Meneses200196-229)

Other national groups fought alongside the Nationalists. Despite the Irish government's prohibition against participating in the war, about 600 followers of [Eoin O'Duffy](/wiki/Eoin_O%27Duffy "Eoin O'Duffy") and [Fine Gael](/wiki/Fine_Gael "Fine Gael") formed the ["Irish Brigade"](/wiki/Irish_Brigade_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Irish Brigade (Spanish Civil War)") to fight for Franco.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961635-230) The majority of them were anti-Communist Catholics.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova2010225-231)[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMittermaier2010195-232) Around 150 to 170 [White Russians](/wiki/White_movement "White movement") fought for Franco.[[226]](#cite_note-233) Romanian volunteers and legionaries of the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard") allied their movement with the Nationalists.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOthen2008102-234)

### Support for the Republicans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Support for the Republicans")]

Main articles: [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades"), [Polish volunteers in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Polish_volunteers_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Polish volunteers in the Spanish Civil War"), and [Polish arms sales to Republican Spain](/wiki/Polish_arms_sales_to_Republican_Spain "Polish arms sales to Republican Spain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H28510%2C_Spanien%2C_Gefechtsstand_des_Etkar_Andr%C3%A9_Bataillons.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H28510%2C_Spanien%2C_Gefechtsstand_des_Etkar_Andr%C3%A9_Bataillons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H28510,_Spanien,_Gefechtsstand_des_Etkar_Andr%C3%A9_Bataillons.jpg)

The Etkar André battalion of the [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades")

Volunteers from many countries fought in Spain, most of them for the Republicans. About 32,000[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001942-235) fought in the [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades"). Perhaps another 3,000[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001942-235) fought as members of the [Confederación Nacional del Trabajo](/wiki/Confederaci%C3%B3n_Nacional_del_Trabajo "Confederación Nacional del Trabajo") (CNT) and the Workers' Party of Marxist Unification ([POUM](/wiki/POUM "POUM")) militias. Those fighting with POUM most famously included [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") and the small [ILP Contingent](/wiki/ILP_Contingent "ILP Contingent"). Around 2,000 Portuguese leftists fought for the Republicans and were spread throughout different units.[[229]](#cite_note-236) Many of the non-Spaniards were affiliated with radical communist or socialist entities and believed that the Spanish Republic was a front line in the war against fascism. The units represented the largest foreign contingent of those fighting for the Republicans. Roughly 40,000 foreign nationals fought with the Brigades, though no more than 18,000 were in the conflict at any given time. They claimed to represent 53 nations.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961637-237) Significant numbers came from [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") (10,000), Nazi Germany and [Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria") (5,000), and [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") (3,350). More than 1,000 each came from the Soviet Union, the United States, the United Kingdom, [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%9346) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–46)") and Canada.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961637-237) The [Thälmann Battalion](/wiki/Th%C3%A4lmann_Battalion "Thälmann Battalion"), a group of Germans, and the [Garibaldi Battalion](/wiki/Garibaldi_Battalion "Garibaldi Battalion"), a group of Italians, distinguished their units during the [siege of Madrid](/wiki/Siege_of_Madrid "Siege of Madrid"). Americans fought in units such as the [XV International Brigade](/wiki/XV_International_Brigade "XV International Brigade") ("Abraham Lincoln Brigade"), while Canadians joined the [Mackenzie–Papineau Battalion](/wiki/Mackenzie%E2%80%93Papineau_Battalion "Mackenzie–Papineau Battalion").[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961638–639-238)

The International Brigades were supported by Communists worldwide, beginning with a Prague conference to raise a brigade soon after the July revolt, followed by a full-scale propaganda campaign for the Popular Front. Communist parties worldwide launched a full-scale propaganda campaign for the Popular Front. Leaders of the [Communist International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International") and the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") went to Spain.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichardson198212-239)

Soviet Union [General Secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") broke the Non-Intervention Agreement and League of Nations embargo by providing material assistance to the Republican forces, becoming their only source of major weapons. Unlike Hitler and Mussolini, Stalin tried to do this covertly.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowson1998125-240) Estimates of material provided by the USSR to the Republicans vary between 634 and 806 aircraft, 331 and 362 tanks and 1,034 to 1,895 artillery pieces.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2004156-241) Stalin also created Section X of the Soviet Union military to head the weapons shipment operation, called [Operation X](/w/index.php?title=Operation_X_(Spanish_Civil_War)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Operation X (Spanish Civil War) (page does not exist)"). Despite Stalin's interest in aiding the Republicans, the quality of arms was inconsistent.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2004156–157-242)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006152–153-243) Many rifles and field guns provided were old or obsolete, but the [T-26](/wiki/T-26 "T-26") and [BT-5](/wiki/BT-5 "BT-5") tanks were modern and effective.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2004156–157-242) Their supplied aircraft was current with their own forces but the German aircraft for the Nationalists proved superior by the end of the war.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006152-244) The movement of arms from Russia to Spain was extremely slow. Many shipments were lost or incomplete.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowson1998126–129-245) The ships hid arms under false decks. At sea, Soviet captains used [deceptive flags and paint schemes](/wiki/False_flag "False flag") to evade Nationalist detection.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowson1998134-246)

The USSR sent 2,000–3,000 military advisers to Spain; while the Soviet commitment of troops was fewer than 500 men at a time, Soviet volunteers often operated Soviet-made tanks and aircraft, particularly at the beginning of the war.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006163-247)[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200592-248)[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961944-249)[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961637-237) The Soviets also ran the People's Commissariat for Internal Affairs ([NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD")) inside the Republican rearguard. Communist figures led operations that included the murders of leftist figures.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006246,_273-250)[[244]](#cite_note-251)[[245]](#cite_note-252)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Dabrowszczacy_przysiegaja_wiernosc_sprawie_Republiki.jpg/250px-Dabrowszczacy_przysiegaja_wiernosc_sprawie_Republiki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dabrowszczacy_przysiegaja_wiernosc_sprawie_Republiki.jpg)

[Polish volunteers](/wiki/Polish_volunteers_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Polish volunteers in the Spanish Civil War") in the International Brigades

After the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"), Poland was the second largest arms supplier for the Republic and the 4th largest arms supplier when considering Italy and Germany. The [Polish sold arms to Republican Spain](/wiki/Polish_arms_sales_to_Republican_Spain "Polish arms sales to Republican Spain") throughout the war, motivated exclusively by economic interest, as their government favored the Nationalists. Since Poland was bound by non-intervention obligations, Polish governmental officials and the military disguised sales as commercial transactions. The weapons were obsolete, second-rate, and overpriced. Though sales amounted to $40M and up to 7% of overall Republican military spending, in some categories like [machine-guns](/wiki/Machine-guns "Machine-guns"), they might have accounted for 50% of all arms delivered.[[246]](#cite_note-253)

Unlike the United States and major Latin American governments, such as the [ABC nations](/wiki/ABC_nations "ABC nations") and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), the Mexican government supported the Republicans.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006139–140-254)[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006291-255) Mexico abstained from following the French-British non-intervention proposals,[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006139–140-254) and provided $2,000,000 in aid and material assistance, which included 20,000 rifles and 20 million cartridges.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006139–140-254) Mexico provided diplomatic help and arranged sanctuary for some 50,000 Republican refugees including Spanish intellectuals and orphaned children from Republican families.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006412–413-256)

French Prime Minister [Léon Blum](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Blum "Léon Blum") was sympathetic to the republic,[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlpert199414-257) fearing that the success of Nationalist forces in Spain would create an alliance with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy, encircling France.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlpert199414-257) After initially declaring that they would not aid the Republicans[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlpert199420–23-258) and signing the Non-Intervention Agreement,[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlpert199443-259) France provided the Republicans with aircraft,[[253]](#cite_note-260)[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlpert199446–47-261) fighter pilots and engineers to help the Republicans.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWerstein1969139-262) Covert support ended by December 1936 but the spectre of French intervention against the Nationalists remained a serious possibility throughout the war.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne200828-263)[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELukešGoldstein1999176-264)

## Course of the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Course of the war")]

See also: [List of battles in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/List_of_battles_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "List of battles in the Spanish Civil War")

### 1936

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 1936")]

Main article: [1936 in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/1936_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "1936 in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_September_1936.png/500px-Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_September_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_September_1936.png)

Map showing Spain in September 1936:

Area under Nationalist control

Area under Republican control

A large [air](/wiki/Spanish_airlift_of_1936 "Spanish airlift of 1936") and [sealift](/wiki/Sealift "Sealift") of Nationalist troops in Spanish Morocco was organised to the southwest of Spain.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200671-265) Coup leader Sanjurjo was killed in a plane crash on 20 July,[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200696-266)[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961162-267) leaving an effective command split between Mola in the North and Franco in the South.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston19834–10-91) This period also saw the worst actions of the so-called "[Red](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Spain) "Red Terror (Spain)")" and "[White Terrors](/wiki/White_Terror_(Spain) "White Terror (Spain)")" in Spain.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200681–94-268) On 21 July, the fifth day of the rebellion, the Nationalists captured the central [Spanish naval base](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy"), located in [Ferrol, Galicia](/wiki/Ferrol,_Galicia "Ferrol, Galicia").[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200673–74-269)

A rebel force under Colonel [Alfonso Beorlegui Canet](/wiki/Alfonso_Beorlegui_Canet "Alfonso Beorlegui Canet"), sent by General Mola and Colonel Esteban García, undertook the [Campaign of Gipuzkoa](/wiki/Campaign_of_Gipuzkoa "Campaign of Gipuzkoa") from July to September. The capture of [Gipuzkoa](/wiki/Gipuzkoa "Gipuzkoa") isolated the Republican provinces in the north. On 5 September, the Nationalists closed the French border to the Republicans in the [battle of Irún](/wiki/Battle_of_Ir%C3%BAn "Battle of Irún").[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006116–117-270) On 15 September [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián"), home to a divided Republican force of anarchists and Basque nationalists, was taken by Nationalist soldiers.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006116-271)

The Republic proved ineffective militarily, relying on disorganised revolutionary militias. The Republican government under Giral resigned on 4 September, unable to cope with the situation, and was replaced by a mostly Socialist organisation under [Francisco Largo Caballero](/wiki/Francisco_Largo_Caballero "Francisco Largo Caballero").[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006144-272) The new leadership began to unify central command in the republican zone.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006146–147-273) The civilian militias were often simply just civilians armed with whatever was available. Thus, they fared poorly in combat, particularly against the professional Army of Africa armed with modern weapons, ultimately contributing to Franco's rapid advance.[[267]](#cite_note-274)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Surrender_of_Red_Soldiers%2C_Somosierra%2C_Madrid_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Surrender_of_Red_Soldiers%2C_Somosierra%2C_Madrid_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surrender_of_Red_Soldiers,_Somosierra,_Madrid_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Surrender of Republican soldiers in the Somosierra area, 1936

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Funeral_of_Leonese_anarquist_Buenaventura_Durruti_in_Barcelona%2C_there_was_Llu%C3%ADs_Companys_and_Joan_Garc%C3%ADa_Oliver.jpg/250px-Funeral_of_Leonese_anarquist_Buenaventura_Durruti_in_Barcelona%2C_there_was_Llu%C3%ADs_Companys_and_Joan_Garc%C3%ADa_Oliver.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funeral_of_Leonese_anarquist_Buenaventura_Durruti_in_Barcelona,_there_was_Llu%C3%ADs_Companys_and_Joan_Garc%C3%ADa_Oliver.jpg)

[Leonese anarchist Buenaventura Durruti was fatally shot](/wiki/Death_and_funeral_of_Buenaventura_Durruti "Death and funeral of Buenaventura Durruti") after he arrived in Madrid to reinforce the morale of the Republicans during an unsuccessful Francoist siege in Madrid.[[268]](#cite_note-:0-275) His funeral, headed (in the image) by [Lluís Companys](/wiki/Llu%C3%ADs_Companys "Lluís Companys"), president of the [Generalitat of Catalonia](/wiki/Generalitat_of_Catalonia "Generalitat of Catalonia"), and [Joan García i Oliver](/wiki/Juan_Garc%C3%ADa_Oliver "Juan García Oliver"), [Minister of Justice](/wiki/Minister_of_Justice "Minister of Justice") of the [Spanish Republic](/wiki/Spanish_Republic "Spanish Republic"), was in Barcelona.[[269]](#cite_note-276)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/F%C3%A0brica_d%27armes_d%27Alginet_%28Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0%29.jpg/250px-F%C3%A0brica_d%27armes_d%27Alginet_%28Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A0brica_d%27armes_d%27Alginet_(Pa%C3%ADs_Valenci%C3%A0).jpg)

Alginet weapons factory

On the Nationalist side, Franco was chosen as chief military commander at a meeting of ranking generals at [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca "Salamanca") on 21 September, now called by the title *[Generalísimo](/wiki/General%C3%ADsimo "Generalísimo")*.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston19834–10-91)[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006143-277) Franco won another victory on 27 September when his troops relieved the [siege of the Alcázar](/wiki/Siege_of_the_Alc%C3%A1zar "Siege of the Alcázar") in [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain"),[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006143-277) which had been held by a Nationalist garrison under [Colonel José Moscardó Ituarte](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Moscard%C3%B3_Ituarte "José Moscardó Ituarte") since the beginning of the rebellion, resisting thousands of Republican troops, who completely surrounded the isolated building. Moroccans and elements of the Spanish Legion came to the rescue.[[271]](#cite_note-278) Two days after relieving the siege, Franco proclaimed himself *[Caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")* ("chieftain", the Spanish equivalent of the Italian [Duce](/wiki/Duce "Duce") and the German [Führer](/wiki/F%C3%BChrer "Führer")—meaning: 'director') while forcibly unifying the various and diverse Falangist, Royalist and other elements within the Nationalist cause.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006144-272) The diversion to Toledo gave Madrid time to prepare a defense but was hailed as a major propaganda victory and personal success for Franco.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006121-279) On 1 October 1936, General Franco was confirmed head of state and armies in Burgos. A similar dramatic success for the Nationalists occurred on 17 October, when troops coming from Galicia relieved the besieged town of [Oviedo](/wiki/Oviedo "Oviedo"), in Northern Spain.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova2010109-280)[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECleugh196290-281)

In October, the Francoist troops launched a major offensive toward Madrid,[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006150-282) reaching it in early November and launching a major assault on the city on 8 November.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006177-283) The Republican government was forced to shift from Madrid to Valencia, outside the combat zone, on 6 November.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006171-284) However, the Nationalists' attack on the capital was repulsed in fierce fighting between 8 and 23 November. A contributory factor in the successful Republican defense was the effectiveness of the [Fifth Regiment](/wiki/Fifth_Regiment "Fifth Regiment")[[278]](#cite_note-5º_Regimiento-285) and later the arrival of the International Brigades, though only an approximate 3,000 foreign volunteers participated in the battle.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006177–183-286) On 19 November, [Buenaventura Durruti, a major Spanish anarchist, was fatally shot with unclear causes](/wiki/Death_and_funeral_of_Buenaventura_Durruti "Death and funeral of Buenaventura Durruti").[[268]](#cite_note-:0-275)

Having failed to take the capital, Franco bombarded it from the air and, in the following two years, mounted several offensives to try to encircle Madrid, beginning the three-year [siege of Madrid](/wiki/Siege_of_Madrid "Siege of Madrid"). The [Second Battle of the Corunna Road](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_the_Corunna_Road "Second Battle of the Corunna Road"), a Nationalist offensive to the northwest, pushed Republican forces back, but failed to isolate Madrid. The battle lasted into January.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006191–192-287)

### 1937

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 1937")]

Main article: [1937 in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/1937_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "1937 in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_October_1937.png/500px-Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_October_1937.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_October_1937.png)

Map showing Spain in October 1937:

Area under Nationalist control

Area under Republican control

With his ranks swelled by Italian troops and Spanish colonial soldiers from Morocco, Franco made another attempt to capture Madrid in January and February 1937, but was again unsuccessful. The [Battle of Málaga](/wiki/Battle_of_M%C3%A1laga_(1937) "Battle of Málaga (1937)") started in mid-January, and this Nationalist offensive in Spain's southeast would turn into a disaster for the Republicans, who were poorly organised and armed. The city was taken by Franco on 8 February.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006200–201-288) The consolidation of various militias into the Republican Army had started in December 1936.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006202-289) The main Nationalist advance to cross the [Jarama](/wiki/Jarama "Jarama") and cut the supply to Madrid by the Valencia road, termed the [Battle of Jarama](/wiki/Battle_of_Jarama "Battle of Jarama"), led to heavy casualties (6,000–20,000) on both sides. The operation's main objective was not met, though Nationalists gained a modest amount of territory.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006208–215-290)

A similar Nationalist offensive, the [Battle of Guadalajara](/wiki/Battle_of_Guadalajara "Battle of Guadalajara"), was a more significant defeat for Franco and his armies. This was the only publicised Republican victory of the war. Franco used Italian troops and [blitzkrieg](/wiki/Blitzkrieg "Blitzkrieg") tactics; while many strategists blamed Franco for the rightists' defeat, the Germans believed it was the former at fault for the Nationalists' 5,000 casualties and loss of valuable equipment.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006216–221-291) The German strategists successfully argued that the Nationalists needed to concentrate on vulnerable areas first.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006222-292)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H25224%2C_Guernica%2C_Ruinen.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H25224%2C_Guernica%2C_Ruinen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-H25224,_Guernica,_Ruinen.jpg)

Ruins of [Guernica](/wiki/Guernica "Guernica")

The "War in the North" began in mid-March, with the [Biscay Campaign](/wiki/Biscay_Campaign "Biscay Campaign"). The Basques suffered most from the lack of a suitable air force.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006223–229-293) On 26 April, the Condor Legion bombed the town of [Guernica](/wiki/Guernica "Guernica"), killing 200–300 and causing significant damage. The [bombing of Guernica](/wiki/Bombing_of_Guernica "Bombing of Guernica") had a significant effect on international opinion. The Basques retreated from the area.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006231–233-294)

April and May saw the [May Days](/wiki/May_Days "May Days"), infighting among Republican groups in Catalonia. The dispute was between an ultimately victorious government—Communist forces and the anarchist CNT. The disturbance pleased Nationalist command, but little was done to exploit Republican divisions.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006263–273-155) After the fall of Guernica, the Republican government began to fight back with increasing effectiveness. In July, it made a [move to recapture](/wiki/Segovia_Offensive "Segovia Offensive") [Segovia](/wiki/Segovia "Segovia"), forcing Franco to delay his advance on the Bilbao front, but for only two weeks. The [Huesca Offensive](/wiki/Huesca_Offensive "Huesca Offensive") failed similarly.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006277-295)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Bro%C5%84_pancerna_armii_republika%C5%84skiej_%281-E-6728%29.jpg/250px-Bro%C5%84_pancerna_armii_republika%C5%84skiej_%281-E-6728%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bro%C5%84_pancerna_armii_republika%C5%84skiej_(1-E-6728).jpg)

Republican T-26 tank on the Aragon front, November 1937.

Mola, Franco's second-in-command, was killed on 3 June, in an airplane accident.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006235-296) In early July, despite the earlier loss at the [Battle of Bilbao](/wiki/Battle_of_Bilbao "Battle of Bilbao"), the government launched a strong counter-offensive to the west of Madrid, focusing on [Brunete](/wiki/Brunete "Brunete"). The [Battle of Brunete](/wiki/Battle_of_Brunete "Battle of Brunete"), however, was a significant defeat for the Republic, which lost many of its most accomplished troops. The offensive led to an advance of 50 square kilometres (19 mi2), and left 25,000 Republican casualties.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006277–284-297)

A Republican offensive against [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza") was also a failure. Despite having land and aerial advantages, the [Battle of Belchite](/wiki/Battle_of_Belchite_(1937) "Battle of Belchite (1937)"), a place lacking any military interest, resulted in an advance of only 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) and the loss of much equipment.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006296–299-298) Franco invaded [Aragón](/wiki/Arag%C3%B3n "Aragón") and [took the city of Santander](/wiki/Battle_of_Santander "Battle of Santander") in [Cantabria](/wiki/Cantabria "Cantabria") in August.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006237-299) With the surrender of the Republican army in the Basque territory came the [Santoña Agreement](/wiki/Santo%C3%B1a_Agreement "Santoña Agreement").[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006237–238-300) [Gijón](/wiki/Gij%C3%B3n "Gijón") finally fell in late October in the [Asturias Offensive](/wiki/Asturias_Offensive "Asturias Offensive").[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006302-301) Franco had effectively won in the north. At November's end, with Franco's troops closing in on Valencia, the government had to move again, this time to Barcelona.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1973-188)

### 1938

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 1938")]

Main article: [1938–1939 in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/1938%E2%80%931939_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "1938–1939 in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_July_1938.png/500px-Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_July_1938.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_July_1938.png)

Map showing Spain in July 1938:

Area under Nationalist control

Area under Republican control

The [Battle of Teruel](/wiki/Battle_of_Teruel "Battle of Teruel") was an important confrontation in 1938, its outcome heralding future progress of the war. The city, which had formerly belonged to the Nationalists, was conquered by Republicans in January. Francoist troops launched a counter-offensive and recovered the city by 22 February, the Nationalists relying heavily on German and Italian air support.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006315–322-302)

Teruel secured, on 7 March the Nationalists launched the [Aragon Offensive](/wiki/Aragon_Offensive "Aragon Offensive"); by 14 April they had pushed east through to the Mediterranean, cutting the Republican-held portion of Spain in two. The Republican government attempted to sue for peace in May,[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961820–821-303) but Franco demanded unconditional surrender, and the war raged on.

In July, the Nationalist army [pressed southward](/wiki/Levante_Offensive "Levante Offensive") from Teruel, pushing south along the coast toward the capital of the Republic at Valencia, but was halted in heavy fighting along the [XYZ Line](/wiki/XYZ_Line "XYZ Line"), a system of fortifications defending Valencia.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006346–347-304)
The Republican government then launched an all-out campaign to reconnect their territory in the [Battle of the Ebro](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Ebro "Battle of the Ebro"), from 24 July until 26 November; the scale of the Republican offensive forced Franco to personally take command.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006349–359-305)

The Republican Ebro campaign was unsuccessful, undermined by the [agreement signed in Munich, Germany, between Hitler and Chamberlain](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement"). The Munich Agreement effectively caused a collapse in Republican morale by ending hope of an anti-fascist alliance with Western powers.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006362-306) The subsequent Republican retreat from the Ebro all but determined the outcome of the war.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006349–359-305) Eight days before the new year, Franco threw massive forces into an [invasion of Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia_Offensive "Catalonia Offensive").[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006374-307)

### 1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 1939")]

Main article: [1938–1939 in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/1938%E2%80%931939_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "1938–1939 in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_February_1939.png/500px-Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_February_1939.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War_in_February_1939.png)

Map showing Spain in February 1939:

Area under Nationalist control

Area under Republican control

Franco's troops conquered Catalonia in a whirlwind campaign during the first two months of 1939. [Tarragona](/wiki/Tarragona "Tarragona") fell on 15 January,[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006376-308) followed by [Barcelona on 26 January](/wiki/Fall_of_Barcelona_(1939) "Fall of Barcelona (1939)")[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006378-309) and [Girona](/wiki/Girona "Girona") on 2 February.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006380-310) On 27 February, the United Kingdom and France recognized the Franco regime.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200686-311)

Only Madrid and a few other strongholds remained for the Republican forces. On 5 March 1939 the Republican army, led by the Colonel [Segismundo Casado](/wiki/Segismundo_Casado "Segismundo Casado") and the politician [Julián Besteiro](/wiki/Juli%C3%A1n_Besteiro "Julián Besteiro"), rose against the prime minister Juan Negrín and formed the [National Defence Council](/wiki/National_Defence_Council_(Spain) "National Defence Council (Spain)") *(Consejo Nacional de Defensa* or *CND)* to negotiate a peace deal.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006391–392-312) Negrín fled to France on 6 March,[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961879–882-313) but the Communist troops around Madrid rose against the junta, starting a brief civil war within the civil war.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006256-27) Casado defeated them, and began peace negotiations with the Nationalists, but Franco refused to accept anything less than unconditional surrender.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006394–395-314)

On 26 March, the Nationalists started a general offensive, on 28 March the Nationalists occupied Madrid and, by 31 March, they controlled all Spanish territory.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006396–397-315) Franco [proclaimed victory](/wiki/Final_offensive_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Final offensive of the Spanish Civil War") in a radio speech aired on 1 April, when the last of the Republican forces surrendered.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDerby200928-316)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Francisco_Franco_escoltado_por_la_Guardia_Mora_visita_San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_una_vez_finalizada_la_guerra_%288_de_8%29_-_Fondo_Mar%C3%ADn-Kutxa_Fototeka.jpg/250px-Francisco_Franco_escoltado_por_la_Guardia_Mora_visita_San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_una_vez_finalizada_la_guerra_%288_de_8%29_-_Fondo_Mar%C3%ADn-Kutxa_Fototeka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francisco_Franco_escoltado_por_la_Guardia_Mora_visita_San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_una_vez_finalizada_la_guerra_(8_de_8)_-_Fondo_Mar%C3%ADn-Kutxa_Fototeka.jpg)

Franco arriving in San Sebastian in 1939

After the end of the war, there were harsh reprisals against Franco's former enemies.[[310]](#cite_note-317) Thousands of Republicans were imprisoned and at least 30,000 executed.[[311]](#cite_note-318) Other estimates of these deaths range from 50,000[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006405-319) to 200,000, depending on which deaths are included. Many others were put to [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour"), building railways, draining swamps, and digging canals.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006405-319)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Spain_final-guerra-civil.jpg/250px-Spain_final-guerra-civil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spain_final-guerra-civil.jpg)

Franco declares the end of the war, though small pockets of Republicans fought on.

At the end of the war, in what was called [La Retirada](/wiki/La_Retirada "La Retirada") (withdrawal) hundreds of thousands of Republicans fled abroad, with some 500,000 fleeing to France.[[313]](#cite_note-320) Refugees were confined in [internment camps](/wiki/Concentration_camps_in_France "Concentration camps in France") of the French Third Republic, such as [Camp Gurs](/wiki/Camp_Gurs "Camp Gurs") or [Camp Vernet](/wiki/Camp_Vernet "Camp Vernet"), where 12,000 Republicans were housed in squalid conditions. In his capacity as consul in Paris, Chilean poet and politician [Pablo Neruda](/wiki/Pablo_Neruda "Pablo Neruda") organised the immigration to [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") of 2,200 Republican exiles.[[314]](#cite_note-321)

Of the 17,000 refugees housed in Gurs, farmers and others who could not find relations in France were encouraged by the Third Republic, in agreement with the Francoist government, to return to Spain. The great majority did so and were turned over to the Francoist authorities in [Irún](/wiki/Ir%C3%BAn "Irún").[[315]](#cite_note-documentary-322) From there, they were transferred to the [Miranda de Ebro](/wiki/Miranda_de_Ebro "Miranda de Ebro") camp for "purification" according to the [Law of Political Responsibilities](/wiki/Law_of_Political_Responsibilities "Law of Political Responsibilities"). After the proclamation by Marshal [Philippe Pétain](/wiki/Philippe_P%C3%A9tain "Philippe Pétain") of the [Vichy regime](/wiki/Vichy_regime "Vichy regime"), the refugees became political prisoners, and the French police attempted to round up those who had been liberated from the camp. Along with other "undesirable" people, the Spaniards were sent to the [Drancy internment camp](/wiki/Drancy_internment_camp "Drancy internment camp") before being deported to [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). About 5,000 Spaniards died in the [Mauthausen concentration camp](/wiki/Mauthausen_concentration_camp "Mauthausen concentration camp").[[315]](#cite_note-documentary-322)

After the official end of the war, [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") was waged on an irregular basis by the [Spanish Maquis](/wiki/Spanish_Maquis "Spanish Maquis") well into the 1950s, gradually reduced by military defeats and scant support from the exhausted population. In 1944, a group of republican veterans, who also fought in the [French resistance](/wiki/French_resistance "French resistance") against the Nazis, invaded the [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran") in northwest Catalonia, but were defeated after 10 days.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006421–422-323) According to some scholars, the Spanish Civil War lasted until 1952; until 1939 it was "conventional civil war", but afterwards it turned into an "irregular civil war".[[317]](#cite_note-324)

## Evacuation of children

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Evacuation of children")]

Main article: [Evacuation of children in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Evacuation_of_children_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Evacuation of children in the Spanish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Spanish_War_Children_%28restored%29.png/250px-Spanish_War_Children_%28restored%29.png)](/wiki/File:Spanish_War_Children_(restored).png)

Children preparing for evacuation, some giving the Republican salute. The Republicans showed a [raised fist](/wiki/Raised_fist "Raised fist") whereas the Nationalists gave the [Roman salute](/wiki/Roman_salute "Roman salute").[[note 4]](#cite_note-325)

The Republicans oversaw the evacuation of 30,000–35,000 children from their zone,[[318]](#cite_note-kowalsky-326) starting with Basque areas, from which 20,000 were evacuated. Their destinations included the United Kingdom[[319]](#cite_note-327) and the USSR, and many other countries in Europe, along with [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"). The policy of evacuating children to foreign countries was initially opposed to by elements in the government as well as private charities, who saw the policy as unnecessary and harmful to the well-being of the evacuated children.[[318]](#cite_note-kowalsky-326) On 21 May 1937, around 4,000 Basque children were evacuated to the UK on the aging steamship SS *Habana* from the Spanish port of [Santurtzi](/wiki/Santurtzi "Santurtzi"). Upon their arrival two days later in [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), the children were sent to families all over England, with over 200 children accommodated in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales").[[320]](#cite_note-328) The upper age limit was initially set at 12 but raised to 15.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuchanan1997109–110-329) By mid-September, all of *los niños vascos*, as they became known,[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESabín-Fernández2010194-330) had found homes with families.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESabín-Fernández2010121-331) Most were repatriated to Spain after the war, but some 250 were still in Britain by the end of the Second World War in 1945 and some chose to settle there.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESabín-Fernández2010125–126-332)

## Financing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Financing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/1937_BandoNacional_billete1peseta_Burgos_escudomonarquia.jpg/250px-1937_BandoNacional_billete1peseta_Burgos_escudomonarquia.jpg)](/wiki/File:1937_BandoNacional_billete1peseta_Burgos_escudomonarquia.jpg)

One-peseta Nationalist note, 1937

During the Civil War the Nationalist and Republican military expenditures combined totalled some $3.89bn, on average $1.44bn annually.[[note 5]](#cite_note-333) The overall Nationalist expenditures are calculated at $2.04bn, while the Republican ones reached ca. $1,85bn.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334) In comparison, in 1936–1938 the French military expenditure totalled $0.87bn, the Italian ones reached $2.64bn, and the British ones stood at $4.13bn.[[326]](#cite_note-335) As in the mid-1930s the Spanish GDP was much smaller than the Italian, French or British ones,[[327]](#cite_note-336) and as in the Second Republic the annual defence and security budget was usually around $0,13bn (total annual governmental spendings were close to $0.65bn),[[note 6]](#cite_note-337) wartime military expenditures put huge strain on the Spanish economy. Financing the war posed enormous challenge for both the Nationalists and the Republicans.

The two combatant parties followed similar financial strategies; in both cases money creation, rather than new taxes or issue of debt, was key to financing the war.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334)

Both sides relied mostly on domestic resources; in the case of the Nationalists, they amounted to 63% of the overall spendings ($1.28bn) and in the case of the Republicans they stood at 59% ($1.09bn). In the Nationalist zone money creation was responsible for some 69% of domestic resources, while in the Republican one the corresponding figure stood at 60%; it was accomplished mostly by means of advances, credits, loans and debit balances from respective central banks.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334) However, while in the Nationalist zone the rising stock of money was only marginally above the production growth rate, in the Republican zone it by far exceeded dwindling production figures. The result was that while by the end of the war the Nationalist inflation was 41% compared to 1936, the Republican one was in triple digits. The second component of domestic resource was fiscal revenue. In the Nationalist zone it grew steadily and in the 2nd half of 1938 it was 214% of the figure from the 2nd half of 1936.[[328]](#cite_note-338) In the Republican zone fiscal revenues in 1937 dropped to some 25% of revenues recorded in the proportional area in 1935 but recovered slightly in 1938. Neither side re-engineered the pre-war tax system; differences resulted from dramatic problems with tax collection in the Republican zone and from the course of the war, as more and more of the population were governed by the Nationalists. A smaller percentage of domestic resources came from expropriations, donations or internal borrowing.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/1_Peseta_-_Ministerio_de_Hacienda_%281937%29_01.jpg/250px-1_Peseta_-_Ministerio_de_Hacienda_%281937%29_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_Peseta_-_Ministerio_de_Hacienda_(1937)_01.jpg)

One-peseta Republican note, 1937

Foreign resources amounted to 37% in case of the Nationalists ($0,76bn) and 41% in case of the Republicans ($0,77bn).[[note 7]](#cite_note-340) For the Nationalists it was mostly the Italian and German credit;[[note 8]](#cite_note-341) in case of the Republicans, it was sales of gold reserves, mostly to the USSR and in much smaller amount to France. None of the sides resolved to public borrowing and none floated debt on foreign exchange markets.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334)

Authors of recent studies suggest that given Nationalist and Republican spendings were comparable, earlier theory pointing to Republican mismanagement of resources is no longer tenable.[[note 9]](#cite_note-342) Instead, they claim that the Republicans failed to translate their resources into military victory largely because of constraints of the international non-intervention agreement; they were forced to spend in excess of market prices and accept goods of lower quality. Initial turmoil in the Republican zone contributed to problems, while at later stages the course of the war meant that population, territory and resources kept shrinking.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334)

## Friction between Republican leadership and Catalonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Friction between Republican leadership and Catalonia")]

Given the lack of operativeness of the republican army after the [Spanish coup of July 1936](/wiki/Spanish_coup_of_July_1936 "Spanish coup of July 1936"), the columns of militiamen temporarily played their role. There was also an [expedition supported by the Generalitat de Catalunya to recover Mallorca](/wiki/Battle_of_Majorca "Battle of Majorca"). The lack of support from the Spanish government for the underlying cause of the [Generalitat](/wiki/Generalitat_de_Catalunya "Generalitat de Catalunya") being involved into the operation and the Catalanist propaganda that promoted the enlistment of volunteers forced the withdrawal.[[330]](#cite_note-343) Not recapturing Mallorca would be of great importance in the future course of the war.

The [Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic"), prevented from buying weapons abroad by the international agreement of neutrality, which both [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") ignored, urgently required war material. In this context, the Generalitat built a network of [war industries](/wiki/War_Industries_Commission "War Industries Commission") converting civilian industries. When the republican government moved to Barcelona in 1937, it took control of the war industries from the Generalitat. But under their control, production dropped dramatically, with the consequent impact on supplies to the war fronts.[[331]](#cite_note-:02-344)

While this was happening, Prime Minister [Negrín](/wiki/Negr%C3%ADn "Negrín") treated President [Companys](/wiki/Companys "Companys") with notable disloyalty, to the final point of abandoning him at the French border, after appropriating the Generalitat's reserve funds for exile.[[331]](#cite_note-:02-344)

All of above can be illustrated with Negrín's statement collected by [Julián Zugazagoitia](/wiki/Juli%C3%A1n_Zugazagoitia "Julián Zugazagoitia"):[[332]](#cite_note-345)

> I am not waging war against Franco so that a stupid and sleazy separatism will return to us in Barcelona. I am waging war for Spain and for Spain! For greatness and for greatness! Those who assume otherwise are mistaken. There is only one nation: Spain! Before consenting to nationalist campaigns that lead us to dismemberment that I in no way admit, I would give way to Franco without any other condition than to get aside the Germans and Italians.
