The death toll of the Spanish Civil War is far from clear and remains—especially in part related to war and postwar repression—controversial. Many general historiographic works—notably in Spain—refrain from advancing any figures or at best propose general descriptions.[[344]](#cite_note-402)[[345]](#cite_note-403)[[346]](#cite_note-404)[[note 55]](#cite_note-405)[[note 56]](#cite_note-406) Foreign scholars, especially English-speaking historians, are more willing to offer some general estimates, though some have revised their projections, usually downward,[[note 57]](#cite_note-407) and the figures vary from 1 million to 250,000.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Women_pleading_with_Rebels_for_Lives_of_Prisioners%2C_Constantina%2C_Seville_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Women_pleading_with_Rebels_for_Lives_of_Prisioners%2C_Constantina%2C_Seville_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Women_pleading_with_Rebels_for_Lives_of_Prisioners,_Constantina,_Seville_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Women pleading with Nationalists for the lives of prisoners, [Constantina](/wiki/Constantina,_Seville "Constantina, Seville"), 1936

The totals advanced usually include or exclude various categories. Scholars who focus on killings or "violent deaths" most typically list (1) combat and combat-related deaths; figures in this rubric range from 100,000[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson1974412-408)[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuñoz2009412-409) to 700,000;[[349]](#cite_note-410) (2) rearguard terror, both judicial and extrajudicial, recorded until the end of the Civil War: 103,000[[350]](#cite_note-411) to 235,000;[[351]](#cite_note-Jackson_1965,_p._412-412) (3) civilian deaths from military action, typically air raids: 10,000[[351]](#cite_note-Jackson_1965,_p._412-412) to 15,000.[[352]](#cite_note-413) These categories combined point to totals from 235,000[[353]](#cite_note-414) to 715,000.[[354]](#cite_note-415) Many authors opt for a broader view and calculate "death toll" by adding also (4) above-the-norm deaths caused by malnutrition,[[355]](#cite_note-416) hygiene shortcomings, cold, illness, etc. recorded until the end of the Civil War: 30,000[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuñoz2009375-417) to 630,000.[[357]](#cite_note-418) It is not unusual to encounter war statistics which include (5) postwar terror related to Civil War, at times up until 1961: 23,000[[358]](#cite_note-Larrazabal_1977,_pp._428–429-419) to 200,000.[[351]](#cite_note-Jackson_1965,_p._412-412) Some authors also add (6) foreign combat and combat-related deaths: 3,000[[359]](#cite_note-420) to 25,000,[[358]](#cite_note-Larrazabal_1977,_pp._428–429-419) (7) Spaniards killed in World War II: 6,000,[[358]](#cite_note-Larrazabal_1977,_pp._428–429-419) (8) deaths related to postwar guerrilla, typically the [Invasion of Val d'Aran](/wiki/Invasion_of_Val_d%27Aran "Invasion of Val d'Aran"): 4,000,[[358]](#cite_note-Larrazabal_1977,_pp._428–429-419) (9) above-the-norm deaths caused by malnutrition, etc., recorded after the Civil War but related to it: 160,000[[358]](#cite_note-Larrazabal_1977,_pp._428–429-419) to 300,000.[[360]](#cite_note-421)

Demographers instead try to gauge the difference between the total number of deaths recorded during the war and the total that would result from applying annual death averages from the 1926–1935 period; this difference is considered excess death resulting from the war. The figure they arrive at for the 1936–1939 period is 346,000; the figure for 1936–1942, including the years of postwar deaths resulting from terror and war sufferings, is 540,000.[[note 58]](#cite_note-422) Some scholars calculate the war's "population loss" or "demographic impact"; in this case they might include also (10) migration abroad: 160,000[[note 59]](#cite_note-423) to 730,000[[note 60]](#cite_note-424) and (11) decrease in birth rate: 500,000[[note 61]](#cite_note-425) to 570,000.[[note 62]](#cite_note-426)

## Atrocities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Atrocities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par%2C_Burgos.jpg/250px-Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par%2C_Burgos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par,_Burgos.jpg)

Twenty-six republicans were assassinated by [Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco")'s Nationalists at the beginning of the Spanish Civil War, between August and September 1936. This mass grave is located at the small town of [Estépar](/wiki/Est%C3%A9par "Estépar"), in Burgos Province. The excavation occurred in July–August 2014.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG/250px-Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG)](/wiki/File:Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG)

Victims of the [Paracuellos massacre](/wiki/Paracuellos_massacre "Paracuellos massacre") committed by the Republicans. The Republicans committed many acts of torture, murder, and war crimes throughout the war known as the [Red Terror (Spain)](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Spain) "Red Terror (Spain)").

Death totals remain debated. British historian [Antony Beevor](/wiki/Antony_Beevor "Antony Beevor") wrote in his history of the Civil War that Franco's ensuing "[white terror](/wiki/White_Terror_(Spain) "White Terror (Spain)")" resulted in the deaths of 200,000 people and that the "[red terror](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Spain) "Red Terror (Spain)")" killed 38,000.[[361]](#cite_note-427) Julius Ruiz contends that, "Although the figures remain disputed, a minimum of 37,843 executions were carried out in the Republican zone, with a maximum of 150,000 executions (including 50,000 after the war) in [Nationalist Spain](/wiki/Nationalist_Spain "Nationalist Spain")".[[362]](#cite_note-428) Historian Michael Seidman stated that the Nationalists killed approximately 130,000 people and the Republicans approximately 50,000 people.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman201718-429)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Mapadefosas-mjusticia-es.jpg/250px-Mapadefosas-mjusticia-es.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mapadefosas-mjusticia-es.jpg)

Spanish Civil War grave sites. Location of known burial places. Colours refer to the type of intervention that has been carried out. **Green**: No Interventions Undertaken so far. **White**: Missing grave. **Yellow**: Transferred to the [Valle de los Caídos](/wiki/Valle_de_los_Ca%C3%ADdos "Valle de los Caídos"). **Red**: Fully or Partially Exhumed. **Blue star**: Valle de los Caídos. Source: [Ministry of Justice of Spain](http://mapadefosas.mjusticia.es/)

In 2008 a Spanish judge, [Baltasar Garzón](/wiki/Baltasar_Garz%C3%B3n "Baltasar Garzón"), opened an investigation into the executions and disappearances of 114,266 people between 17 July 1936 and December 1951. Among the executions investigated was that of the poet and dramatist [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), whose body has never been found.[[364]](#cite_note-spanjudge-430) Mention of García Lorca's death was forbidden during Franco's regime.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200692-431)

Research since 2016 has started to locate [mass graves](/wiki/Mass_grave "Mass grave"), using a combination of witness testimony, remote sensing and [forensic geophysics](/wiki/Forensic_geophysics "Forensic geophysics") techniques.[[366]](#cite_note-432)

Historians such as [Helen Graham](/wiki/Helen_Graham_(historian) "Helen Graham (historian)"),[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200530-433) [Paul Preston](/wiki/Paul_Preston "Paul Preston"),[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006307-434) [Antony Beevor](/wiki/Antony_Beevor "Antony Beevor"),[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200686–87-31) [Gabriel Jackson](/wiki/Gabriel_Jackson_(hispanist) "Gabriel Jackson (hispanist)")[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson1965305-435) and [Hugh Thomas](/wiki/Hugh_Thomas_(historian) "Hugh Thomas (historian)")[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001268-436) argue that the mass executions behind the Nationalist lines were organised and approved by the Nationalist rebel authorities, while the executions behind the Republican lines were the result of the breakdown of the Republican state and chaos:

> Though there was much wanton killing in rebel Spain, the idea of the *limpieza*, the "cleaning up", of the country from the evils which had overtaken it, was a disciplined policy of the new authorities and a part of their programme of regeneration. In republican Spain, most of the killing was the consequence of anarchy, the outcome of a national breakdown, and not the work of the state, although some political parties in some cities abetted the enormities, and some of those responsible ultimately rose to positions of authority.

— Hugh Thomas[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961268-437)

Conversely, historians such as [Stanley Payne](/wiki/Stanley_Payne "Stanley Payne"), Julius Ruiz[[372]](#cite_note-438) and José Sánchez[[373]](#cite_note-439) argue that the political violence in the Republican zone was in fact organized by the left:

> In general, this was not an irrepressible outpouring of hatred, by the man in the street for his "oppressors", as it has sometimes been painted, but a semi-organized activity carried out by sections of nearly all the leftist groups. In the entire leftist zone the only organized political party that eschewed involvement in such activity were the Basque Nationalists.[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1973650-440)

### Nationalists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Nationalists")]

See also: [White Terror (Spain)](/wiki/White_Terror_(Spain) "White Terror (Spain)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Savoia-Marchetti_SM.81.jpg/250px-Savoia-Marchetti_SM.81.jpg)](/wiki/File:Savoia-Marchetti_SM.81.jpg)

Nationalist [SM.81](/wiki/Savoia-Marchetti_SM.81 "Savoia-Marchetti SM.81") aircraft bomb Madrid in late November 1936.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Children_during_the_Madrid_bombing.jpg/250px-Children_during_the_Madrid_bombing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Children_during_the_Madrid_bombing.jpg)

Children take refuge during the Francoist bombing over [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") (1936–1937). Republicans managed to repel the [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Madrid "Siege of Madrid") despite the bombing.

Nationalist atrocities, which authorities frequently ordered so as to eradicate any trace of "leftism" in Spain, were common. The notion of a *limpieza* (cleansing) formed an essential part of the rebel strategy, and the process began immediately after an area had been captured.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200698-441) Estimates of the death toll vary; historian Paul Preston estimates the minimum number of those executed by the rebels as 130,000,[[376]](#cite_note-442) while Antony Beevor places the figure much higher at an estimated 200,000 dead.[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200694-443) The violence was carried out in the rebel zone by the military, the Civil Guard and the Falange in the name of the regime.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200688–89-444) Julius Ruiz reports that the Nationalists killed 100,000 people during the war and executed at least 28,000 immediately after. The first three months of the war were the bloodiest, with 50 to 70 percent of all executions carried out by Franco's regime, from 1936 to 1975, occurring during this period.[[379]](#cite_note-445)

Many such acts were committed by reactionary groups during the first weeks of the war.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200688–89-444) This included the execution of schoolteachers,[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200689-446) because the efforts of the Second Spanish Republic to promote [laicism](/wiki/La%C3%AFcit%C3%A9 "Laïcité") and displace the Church from schools by closing religious educational institutions were considered by the Nationalists as an attack on the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"). Extensive killings of civilians were carried out in the cities captured by the Nationalists,[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006121-447) along with the execution of unwanted individuals. These included [non-combatants](/wiki/Non-combatant "Non-combatant") such as [trade-unionists](/wiki/Trade-unionists "Trade-unionists"), Popular Front politicians, suspected [Freemasons](/wiki/Suppression_of_Freemasonry "Suppression of Freemasonry"), Basque, Catalan, [Andalusian](/wiki/Andalusian_nationalism "Andalusian nationalism"), and [Galician](/wiki/Galicianism_(Galicia) "Galicianism (Galicia)") Nationalists, Republican intellectuals, relatives of known Republicans, and those suspected of voting for the Popular Front.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200688–89-444)[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson1965377-448)[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001253–255-449)[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantosCasanovaSolé_I_SabatéVillarroya1999229-450)[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006120–123-451) The Nationalists also frequently killed military officers who refused to support them in the early days of the coup.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200523–24-452) Many killings in the first few months were often done by vigilantes and civilian death squads, with the Nationalist leadership often condoning their actions or even assisting them.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200530–31-453) Post-war executions were conducted by military tribunal, though the accused had limited ways to defend themselves. A large number of the executed were done so for their political activities or positions they held under the Republic during the war, though those who committed their own killings under the Republic were also amongst executed as well.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2012110-454) A 2010 analysis of Catalonia argued that Nationalist executions were more likely to occur when they occupied an area that experienced greater prior violence, likely due to pro-Nationalist civilians seeking revenge for earlier actions by denouncing others to the Nationalist forces.[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcells2010291–313-455) Michael Seidman argues that the Nationalists' greater death toll may be partially attributable to their military success resulting in territorial gains and thus more opportunities to enact violence against their enemies.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman2011172-456) However, during the war, executions declined as the Francoist state began to establish itself.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERuiz2005171–191-457)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Barcelona_bombing_%281938%29.jpg/250px-Barcelona_bombing_%281938%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barcelona_bombing_(1938).jpg)

Bombing in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), 1938

Nationalist forces massacred civilians in Seville, where some 8,000 people were shot; 10,000 were killed in [Cordoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain"); 6,000–12,000 were killed in [Badajoz](/wiki/Badajoz "Badajoz")[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200691-458) after more than 1,000 landowners and conservatives were killed by the revolutionaries. In Granada, where working-class neighbourhoods were hit with artillery and right-wing squads were given free rein to kill government sympathizers,[[393]](#cite_note-459) at least 2,000 people were murdered.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200689-446) In February 1937, over 7,000 were killed after the capture of [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga").[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200693-460) When Bilbao was conquered, thousands of people were sent to prison. There were fewer executions than usual, however, because of the effect Guernica left on Nationalists' reputations internationally.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006236–237-461) The numbers killed as the columns of the Army of Africa devastated and pillaged their way between Seville and Madrid are particularly difficult to calculate.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006302-462) Landowners who owned the large estates of Southern Spain rode alongside the Army of Africa to reclaim via force of arms the land given to the landless peasants by the Republican government. Rural workers were executed, and it was mockingly joked that they had received their "land reform" in the form of a burial plot.[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200532-463)

Nationalists also murdered Catholic clerics. In one particular incident, following the capture of [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao"), they took hundreds of people, including 16 priests who had served as chaplains for the Republican forces, to the countryside or graveyards and murdered them.[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBieterBieter200391-464)[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200682–83-465)

Franco's forces also persecuted Protestants, including murdering 20 Protestant ministers.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor20068-466) Franco's forces were determined to remove the "Protestant heresy" from Spain.[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman2011205-467) The Nationalists also persecuted Basques, as they strove to eradicate Basque culture.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006237-299) According to Basque sources, some 22,000 Basques were murdered by Nationalists immediately after the Civil War.[[402]](#cite_note-468)

The Nationalist side conducted [aerial bombing of cities](/wiki/Aerial_bombing_of_cities "Aerial bombing of cities") in Republican territory, carried out mainly by the Luftwaffe volunteers of the Condor Legion and the [Italian air force](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica") volunteers of the Corpo Truppe Volontarie: Madrid, [Barcelona](/wiki/Bombing_of_Barcelona "Bombing of Barcelona"), Valencia, [Guernica](/wiki/Bombing_of_Guernica "Bombing of Guernica"), [Durango](/wiki/Bombing_of_Durango "Bombing of Durango"), and other cities were attacked. The Bombing of Guernica was the most controversial.[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestwell200431-469) The Italian air force conducted a particularly heavy bombing raid on Barcelona in early 1938. While some Nationalist leaders did oppose the bombing of the city—for example, Generals Yagüe and Moscardó, who were noted for being nonconformists, protested against the indiscriminate destruction—other Nationalist leaders, often those of a fascist persuasion, saw bombings as necessary to "cleanse" Barcelona.[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman2002156-470)

Michael Seidman observes that the Nationalist terror was a key part of the Nationalist victory as it allowed them to secure their rear; the Russian Whites, in [their civil war](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), had struggled to suppress peasant rebellions, bandits and warlordism behind their lines, while the inability of the Chinese Nationalists to stop banditry during the Chinese Civil War did severe damage to the regime's legitimacy. The Spanish Nationalists, in contrast, imposed a puritanically terrorist order on the populace in their territory. They never suffered from serious partisan activity behind their lines and the fact that banditry did not develop into a serious problem in Spain, despite how easy it would have been in such mountainous terrain, demands explanation. Seidman argues that severe terror, combined with control of the food supply, explains the general lack of guerrilla warfare in the Nationalist rear.[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman201130–31-471) A 2009 analysis of Nationalist violence argues that evidence supports the view that killings were used strategically by the Nationalists to pre-emptively counter potential opposition by targeting individuals and groups deemed most likely to cultivate future rebellions, thus helping the Nationalists win the war.[[406]](#cite_note-472)

### Republicans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Republicans")]

See also: [Red Terror (Spain)](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Spain) "Red Terror (Spain)")

Scholars have estimated that between 38,000[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200681-473) and 70,000[[408]](#cite_note-Dela-474) civilians were killed in Republican-held territories, with the most common estimate being around 50,000.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001900-475)[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006233-476)[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2012244–245-477)[[412]](#cite_note-478)

Whatever the exact number, the death toll was far exaggerated by both sides, for propaganda reasons, giving birth to the legend of the *millón de muertos*.[[note 63]](#cite_note-479) Franco's government would later give names of 61,000 victims of the red terrors, but which are not considered objectively verifiable.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1973-188) The deaths would form the prevailing outside opinion of the republic up until the bombing of Guernica.[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200681-473)

The leftist [Revolution of 1936](/wiki/Revolution_of_1936 "Revolution of 1936") that preceded the war was accompanied since the first months by an escalation of leftist anticlerical terror that, between 18 and 31 July alone, killed 839 religious, continuing during the month of August with 2,055 other victims, including 10 bishops killed, that was 42% of the total number of registered victims in that year.[[413]](#cite_note-480) Particularly noteworthy repression was conducted [in Madrid](/wiki/Republican_repression_in_Madrid_(1936%E2%80%931939) "Republican repression in Madrid (1936–1939)") during the war.

The Republican government was anticlerical, and, when the war began, supporters attacked and murdered Roman Catholic clergy in reaction to the news of military revolt.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200682–83-465) In his 1961 book, Spanish archbishop Antonio Montero Moreno, who at the time was director of the journal *Ecclesia*, wrote that 6,832 were killed during the war, including 4,184 priests, 2,365 monks and friars, and 283 nuns (many were first raped before they died),[[414]](#cite_note-481)[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantosCasanovaSolé_I_SabatéVillarroya199958-482) in addition to 13 bishops, a figure accepted by historians, including Beevor.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200682-483)[[417]](#cite_note-484)[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1973649-485) Some of the killings were carried out with extreme cruelty, some were burned to death, there are reports of castration and disembowelment.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200682-483) Some sources claim that by the conflict's end, 20 percent of the nation's clergy had been killed.[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowen200622-486)[[note 64]](#cite_note-487) The "Execution" of the [Sacred Heart of Jesus](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_of_Jesus "Sacred Heart of Jesus") by Communist militiamen at [Cerro de los Ángeles](/wiki/Cerro_de_los_%C3%81ngeles "Cerro de los Ángeles") near Madrid, on 7 August 1936, was the most infamous of widespread desecration of religious property.[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEalhamRichards200580,_168-488) In dioceses where the Republicans had general control, a large proportion—often a majority—of [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") priests were killed.[[421]](#cite_note-489) Michael Seidman argues that the hatred of the Republicans for the clergy was in excess of anything else; while local revolutionaries might spare the lives of the rich and right-wingers, they seldom offered the same to priests.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman201118–19-88)

Like clergy, civilians were executed in Republican territories. Some civilians were executed as suspected Falangists.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200684-490) Others died in acts of revenge after Republicans heard of massacres carried out in the Nationalist zone.[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200685-491) Even families who simply attended Catholic Mass were hunted down; including children.[[424]](#cite_note-492)[[425]](#cite_note-493) Air raids committed against Republican cities were another driving factor.[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2006-494) Shopkeepers and industrialists were shot if they did not sympathise with the Republicans and were usually spared if they did.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200683-495) Fake justice was sought through [commissions](/wiki/Kangaroo_court "Kangaroo court"), named *checas* after the [Soviet secret police organization](/wiki/Cheka "Cheka").[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200684-490)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Bridge_at_Ronda%2C_Spain.jpg/250px-Bridge_at_Ronda%2C_Spain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bridge_at_Ronda,_Spain.jpg)

The [Puente Nuevo](/wiki/Puente_Nuevo "Puente Nuevo") bridge, Ronda. Both Nationalists and Republicans are claimed to have thrown prisoners from the bridge to their deaths in the canyon.[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961176-496)

Many killings were done by *paseos*, impromptu death squads that emerged as a spontaneous practice amongst revolutionary activists in Republican areas. According to Seidman, the Republican government only made efforts to stop the actions of the *paseos* late in the war; during the first few months, the government either tolerated it or made no efforts to stop it.[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman2017-497) The killings often contained a symbolic element, as those killed were seen as embodying an oppressive source of power and authority. This was also why the Republicans would kill priests or employers who were not considered to personally have done anything wrong but were nonetheless seen as representing the old oppressive order that needed to be destroyed.[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200527-498)

There was infighting between the Republican factions, and the Communists following [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") declared the [Workers' Party of Marxist Unification](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Marxist_Unification "Workers' Party of Marxist Unification") (POUM), an [anti-Stalinist](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist "Anti-Stalinist") [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") party, to be an illegal organization, along with the Anarchists. The Stalinists betrayed and committed mass atrocities on the other Republican factions, such as torture and mass executions. [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell") would record this in his *[Homage to Catalonia](/wiki/Homage_to_Catalonia "Homage to Catalonia")* as well as write *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four")* and *[Animal Farm](/wiki/Animal_Farm "Animal Farm")* to criticize Stalinism.[[431]](#cite_note-499)[[432]](#cite_note-500)
As pressure mounted with the increasing success of the Nationalists, many civilians were executed by councils and tribunals controlled by competing Communist and anarchist groups.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200684-490) Some members of the latter were executed by Soviet-advised communist functionaries in Catalonia,[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961176-496) as recounted by George Orwell's description of the purges in Barcelona in 1937 in which followed a period of increasing tension between competing elements of the Catalan political scene. Some individuals fled to friendly embassies, which would house up to 8,500 people during the war.[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200685-491)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/SpanishLeftistsShootStatueOfChrist.jpg/250px-SpanishLeftistsShootStatueOfChrist.jpg)](/wiki/File:SpanishLeftistsShootStatueOfChrist.jpg)

"Execution" of the [Sacred Heart of Jesus](/wiki/Cerro_de_los_%C3%81ngeles "Cerro de los Ángeles") by Communist militiamen. The photograph in the London *[Daily Mail](/wiki/Daily_Mail "Daily Mail")* had the caption "Spanish Reds' war on religion".[[433]](#cite_note-501)

In the Andalusian town of [Ronda](/wiki/Ronda "Ronda"), 512 suspected Nationalists were executed in the first month of the war.[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1961176-496) Communist [Santiago Carrillo Solares](/wiki/Santiago_Carrillo_Solares "Santiago Carrillo Solares") was accused of the killing of Nationalists in the [Paracuellos massacre](/wiki/Paracuellos_massacre "Paracuellos massacre") near [Paracuellos de Jarama](/wiki/Paracuellos_de_Jarama "Paracuellos de Jarama").[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006172–173-502) Pro-Soviet Communists committed numerous atrocities against fellow Republicans, including other Marxists: [André Marty](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Marty "André Marty"), known as the Butcher of [Albacete](/wiki/Albacete "Albacete"), was responsible for the deaths of some 500 members of the International Brigades.[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006161-503) Andrés Nin, leader of the POUM (Workers' Party of Marxist Unification), and many other prominent POUM members, were murdered by the Communists, with the help of the USSR's NKVD.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006272–273-154)

The Republicans also conducted their own bombing attacks on cities, such as the [bombing of Cabra](/wiki/Bombing_of_Cabra "Bombing of Cabra"); according to [Stanley G. Payne](/wiki/Stanley_G._Payne "Stanley G. Payne"), their attacks were often weak and ineffective.[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2011b219–220-504) Michael Seidman argues that the better trained Nationalist air force was more effective at inflicting casualties, killing an estimated 11,000 civilians compared to approximately 4,000 for the Republican air force.[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeidman2011173-505)

38,000 people were killed in the Republican zone during the war, 17,000 of whom were killed in Madrid or Catalonia within a month of the coup. While the Communists were forthright in their support of extrajudicial killings, much of the Republican side was appalled by the murders.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200687-506) Azaña came close to resigning.[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200685-491) He, alongside other members of Parliament and a great number of other local officials, attempted to prevent Nationalist supporters from being lynched. Some of those in positions of power intervened personally to stop the killings.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200687-506)

## Art and propaganda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Art and propaganda")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Placegeorgeorwell.jpg/250px-Placegeorgeorwell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Placegeorgeorwell.jpg)

In Catalonia, a square near the Barcelona waterfront named *Plaça de George Orwell*

Throughout the Spanish Civil War, people all over the world were exposed to the goings-on and effects of it on its people not only through standard art, but also through [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"). Motion pictures, posters, books, radio programs, and leaflets are a few examples of this media art that was so influential during the war. Produced by both nationalists and republicans, propaganda allowed Spaniards a way to spread awareness about their war all over the world. A film co-produced by famous early-twentieth century authors such as [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway") and [Lillian Hellman](/wiki/Lillian_Hellman "Lillian Hellman") was used as a way to advertise Spain's need for military and monetary aid. This film, *[The Spanish Earth](/wiki/The_Spanish_Earth "The Spanish Earth")*, premiered in America in July 1937. In 1938, [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s *[Homage to Catalonia](/wiki/Homage_to_Catalonia "Homage to Catalonia")*, a personal account of his experiences and observations in the war, was published in the United Kingdom. In 1939, [Jean-Paul Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre") published in France a short story, *[The Wall](/wiki/The_Wall_(Sartre_short_story_collection) "The Wall (Sartre short story collection)")* in which he describes the last night of prisoners of war sentenced to death by shooting.

Leading works of sculpture include Alberto Sánchez Pérez's *El pueblo español tiene un camino que conduce a una estrella* ("The Spanish People Have a Path that Leads to a Star"), a plaster monolith representing the struggle for a socialist utopia;[[439]](#cite_note-museonacional-507) [Julio González's](/wiki/Julio_Gonz%C3%A1lez_(sculptor) "Julio González (sculptor)") *La Montserrat*, an anti-war work which shares its title with a mountain near Barcelona, is created from a sheet of iron which has been hammered and welded to create a peasant mother carrying a small child in one arm and a sickle in the other. and [Alexander Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder")'s *Fuente de mercurio* (Mercury Fountain) a protest work by the American against the Nationalist forced control of Almadén and the mercury mines there.[[440]](#cite_note-montserrat-508)

[Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí") responded to the conflict in his homeland with two powerful oil paintings in 1936: *[Soft Construction with Boiled Beans](/wiki/Soft_Construction_with_Boiled_Beans "Soft Construction with Boiled Beans")*: *A Premonition of Civil War* ([Philadelphia Museum of Art](/wiki/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art "Philadelphia Museum of Art")) and *Autumnal Cannibalism* ([Tate Modern](/wiki/Tate_Modern "Tate Modern"), London).[[441]](#cite_note-Hughes_(2003)-509): 383 p.[[442]](#cite_note-Descharnes_(1984)-510): 223 p.[Joan Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró") created *El Segador* (The Reaper) in 1937, formally titled *El campesino catalán en rebeldía* (Catalan peasant in revolt).[[443]](#cite_note-joan-511) [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") painted [*Guernica*](/wiki/Guernica_(painting) "Guernica (painting)") in 1937, inspired by the bombing of Guernica. The work's size (11 ft by 25.6 ft) grabbed much attention and cast the horrors of the mounting Spanish civil unrest into a global spotlight.[[444]](#cite_note-picasso-512) The painting has since been heralded as an antiwar work and a symbol of peace in the 20th century.[[445]](#cite_note-test-513)

The Army of Africa would feature a place in propaganda on both sides, due to the complex history of the Army and Spanish colonialism in North Africa.[[446]](#cite_note-514)

## Consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Consequences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Nafarroako_irakasleak_1936_1.jpg/250px-Nafarroako_irakasleak_1936_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nafarroako_irakasleak_1936_1.jpg)

Tribute and plaque in memory of murdered or persecuted teachers, [Navarre](/wiki/Navarre "Navarre"), 1936 and later

Costs for the war on both sides were very high. Monetary resources on the Republican side were completely drained from weapons acquisition. On the Nationalist side, the biggest losses came after the conflict, when they had to let Germany exploit the country's mining resources, so until the beginning of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") they barely had the chance to make any profit.[[447]](#cite_note-515)

The political and emotional repercussions of the War transcended the national scale, becoming a precursor to the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2008336-516) The war has frequently been described by historians as the "prelude to" or the "opening round of" the Second World War[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Historian Stanley Payne suggests that this view is an incorrect summary of the geopolitic position of the interwar period, arguing that the Spanish Civil War was a far more clear-cut revolutionary and [counter-revolutionary](/wiki/Counter-revolutionary "Counter-revolutionary") struggle between the left and right wings, while the Second World War initially had fascists and communist powers on the same side with the combined Nazi-Soviet [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"). Payne suggests that instead the civil war was the last of the revolutionary crises that emerged from the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2011a194-517)[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2004313–314-518)

After the War, Spanish policy leaned heavily towards Germany, Portugal and Italy, since they had been the greatest Nationalist supporters and aligned with Spain ideologically. However, the end of the Civil War and later the Second World War saw the isolation of the country from most other nations until the 1950s.[[451]](#cite_note-519)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Civil_War&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The POUM fought in the Spanish Civil War from 17 July 1936 until 16 June 1937, when the POUM was illegalized and suppressed by the Popular Front Republican government led by Prime Minister [Juan Negrín](/wiki/Juan_Negr%C3%ADn "Juan Negrín"), with the government suppression of the POUM supported by [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") and the [PCE](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Spain "Communist Party of Spain").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Basque_2-0)** The Euzko Gudarostea fought in the Spanish Civil War from 17 July 1936 until it surrendered to the Italian [Corpo Truppe Volontarie](/wiki/Corpo_Truppe_Volontarie "Corpo Truppe Volontarie") in the [Santoña Agreement](/wiki/Santo%C3%B1a_Agreement "Santoña Agreement") on 24 August 1937.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-FetJons_3-0)** The only party under [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") from 1937 onward, a [merger of the other factions on the Nationalist side](/wiki/Unification_Decree_(Spain,_1937) "Unification Decree (Spain, 1937)").
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Natmerge_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Natmerge_4-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Natmerge_4-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-Natmerge_4-3) 1936–1937, then merged into [FET y de las JONS](/wiki/FET_y_de_las_JONS "FET y de las JONS")

2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Also known as **The Crusade** (Spanish: *La Cruzada*) or **The Revolution** (Spanish: *La Revolución*) among Nationalists, the **Fourth Carlist War** (Spanish: *Cuarta Guerra Carlista*) among [Carlists](/wiki/Carlists "Carlists"), and **The Rebellion** (Spanish: *La Rebelión*) or **The Uprising** (Spanish: *La Sublevación*) among Republicans.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-206)** See variously: Bennett, Scott, *Radical Pacifism: The War Resisters League and Gandhian Nonviolence in America, 1915–1963*, Syracuse NY, Syracuse University Press, 2003; Prasad, Devi, *War Is a Crime Against Humanity: The Story of War Resisters' International*, London, WRI, 2005. Also see Hunter, Allan, *White Corpsucles in Europe*, Chicago, Willett, Clark & Co., 1939; and Brown, H. Runham, *Spain: A Challenge to Pacifism*, London, The Finsbury Press, 1937.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-325)** "The Roman salute characteristic of Italian fascism was first adopted by the PNE and the JONS, later spreading to the Falange and other extreme right groups, before it became the official salute in Franco's Spain. The JAP salute, which consisted of stretching the right arm horizontally to touch the left shoulder enjoyed only relatively little acceptance. The gesture of the raised fist, so widespread among left-wing workers' groups, gave rise to more regimented variations, such as the salute with the fist on one's temple, characteristic of the German *[Rotfront](/wiki/Rotfrontk%C3%A4mpferbund "Rotfrontkämpferbund")*, which was adopted by the republican Popular Army". The Splintering of Spain, pp. 36–37
5. **[^](#cite_ref-333)** the war lasted 986 days; dollars are quoted at their nominal value of the late 1930s
6. **[^](#cite_ref-337)** in 1934 the Spanish military spendings as reported by the statistical office were 958m ptas; in 1935 they were 1.065m ptas, Huerta Barajas Justo Alberto (2016), *Gobierno u administración militar en la II República Española*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-8434023031](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-8434023031 "Special:BookSources/978-8434023031"), p. 805. The peseta to dolar exchange rate for 1935 varied from 7.32 in August to 7.38 in January, Martínez Méndez P. (1990), *Nuevos datos sobre la evolución de la peseta entre 1900 y 1936*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [8477930724](/wiki/Special:BookSources/8477930724 "Special:BookSources/8477930724"), p. 14
7. **[^](#cite_ref-340)** when assessing financial cost of waging the war, some scholars limit their analysis to foreign resources only and set expenditures of both sides at some $0,7bn each, compare e.g. Romero Salvado, Francisco J. (2013), *Historical Dictionary of the Spanish Civil War*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0810857841](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0810857841 "Special:BookSources/978-0810857841"), p. 20. Similarly, another author claims that "the republican authorities obtained 714 million dollars, and this was the financial cost of the civil war for the Republicans", while "the financial cost of the war on the Francoist side was very similar, between 694 and 716 million dollars".[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova201091-339) The same author claims in the same work that "losing the war cost the Republic almost as much as Franco spent on winning it, some six hundred million dollars on each side" (p. 185)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-341)** exact figures differ; one source claims $0,45bn for Italy and $0,23bn for Germany, Romero Salvado 2013, p. 20; the rest was mostly private credit from British (e.g. [Rio Tinto](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_(corporation) "Rio Tinto (corporation)")) or US (e.g. [Texaco](/wiki/Texaco "Texaco")) companies
9. **[^](#cite_ref-342)** earlier studies suggested that the Republican military expenditures were 4 times larger than the Nationalist ones (40bn ptas v. 12bn ptas); the conclusion drawn was that the Republicans have grossly mismanaged their resources. Recent studies claim that the above figures are calculated in nominal terms, and that entirely different picture emerges when inflation and exchange rates are taken into account,[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartín-AceñaMartínez_RuizPons2012144–165-334)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-346)** highest considered estimate; "la guerra civil fue una espantosa calamidad en la que todas las clases y todos los partidos perdieron. Además del millión o dos milliones de muertos, la salud del pueblo se ha visto minada por su secuela de hambre y enfermedades", Brennan, Gerald (1978), *El laberinto español. Antecedentes sociales y políticos de la guerra civil*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-8485361038](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-8485361038 "Special:BookSources/978-8485361038"), p. 20
11. **[^](#cite_ref-347)** some press estimates from the era, see e.g. "one and a half million Spaniards have already been killed in the war", *[Spain's War Goes On](https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19390328/105/0010)*, [in:] *Daily Record* [Britain] 28 March 1939
12. **[^](#cite_ref-348)** "muertes por causa directa de la Guerra Civil Española"; includes post-war guerilla, Nazi concentration camps, post-war repression, and "sobremortalidad hasta 1943", Jorge L. Tizón, *La guerra como campo de batalla*, Barcelona 2022, ISBN 9788425449635, p. 44
13. **[^](#cite_ref-349)** "esta cruenta lucha le costó a España 1 200 000 muertos entre combatientes y civiles", Pazos Beceiro, Carlos (2004), *La globalización económica neoliberal y la guerra*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-9597071266](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-9597071266 "Special:BookSources/978-9597071266"), p. 116

15. **[^](#cite_ref-351)** Lee, Stephen J. (2000), *European Dictatorships, 1918–1945*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0415230452](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0415230452 "Special:BookSources/978-0415230452"), p. 248; "a reasonable estimate, and a rather conservative one", Howard Griffin, John, Simon, Yves René (1974), *Jacques Maritain: Homage in Words and Pictures*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0873430463](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0873430463 "Special:BookSources/978-0873430463"), p. 11; military casualties only, Ash, Russell (2003), *The Top 10 of Everything 2004*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0789496591](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0789496591 "Special:BookSources/978-0789496591"), p. 68; lowest considered estimate, Brennan (1978), p. 20. The phrase of "one million dead" became a cliche since the 1960s, and many older Spaniards might repeat that "yo siempre había escuchado lo del millon de muertos", compare *burbuja* service, available [here](http://www.burbuja.info/inmobiliaria/politica/175403-muertos-guerra-civil-ii.html). This is so due to extreme popularity of a 1961 novel *Un millón de muertos* by [José María Gironella](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Gironella "José María Gironella"), even though the author many times declared that he had in mind those "muerto espiritualmente", referred after Diez Nicolas, Juan (1985), *La mortalidad en la Guerra Civil Española*, [in:] *Boletín de la Asociación de Demografía Histórica* III/1, p. 42. Scholars claim also that the figure of "one million deaths" was continuously repeated by Francoist authorities "to drive home the point of having saved the country form ruin", Encarnación, Omar G. (2008), *Spanish Politics: Democracy After Dictatorship*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0745639925](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0745639925 "Special:BookSources/978-0745639925"), p. 24, and became one of the "mitos principales del franquismo", referred as "myth no. 9" in Reig Tapia, Alberto (2017), *La crítica de la crítica: Inconsecuentes, insustanciales, impotentes, prepotentes y equidistantes*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-8432318658](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-8432318658 "Special:BookSources/978-8432318658")

Tamames added figures reported in the "violent deaths" rubric for 1936, 1937 and 1938 and 25% of the same category for 1939; then he deducted annual averages for "violent deaths" reported by INE in the mid-1930s to arrive at 149,213. (2000), *European Dictatorships, 1918–1945*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0415230452](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0415230452 "Special:BookSources/978-0415230452"), p. 248; "a reasonable estimate, and a rather conservative one", Howard Griffin, John, Simon, Yves René (1974), *Jacques Maritain: Homage in Words and Pictures*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0873430463](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0873430463 "Special:BookSources/978-0873430463"), p. 11; military casualties only, Ash, Russell (2003), *The Top 10 of Everything 2004*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0789496591](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0789496591 "Special:BookSources/978-0789496591"), p. 68; lowest considered estimate, Brennan (1978), p. 20. The phrase of "one million dead" became a cliche since the 1960s, and many older Spaniards might repeat that "yo siempre había escuchado lo del millon de muertos", compare *burbuja* service, available [here](http://www.burbuja.info/inmobiliaria/politica/175403-muertos-guerra-civil-ii.html). This is so due to extreme popularity of a 1961 novel *Un millón de muertos* by [José María Gironella](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Gironella "José María Gironella"), even though the author many times declared that he had in mind those "muerto espiritualmente", referred after Diez Nicolas, Juan (1985), *La mortalidad en la Guerra Civil Española*, [in:] *Boletín de la Asociación de Demografía Histórica* III/1, p. 42. Scholars claim also that the figure of "one million deaths" was continuously repeated by Francoist authorities "to drive home the point of having saved the country form ruin", Encarnación, Omar G. (2008), *Spanish Politics: Democracy After Dictatorship*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0745639925](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0745639925 "Special:BookSources/978-0745639925"), p. 24, and became one of the "mitos principales del franquismo", referred as "myth no. 9" in Reig Tapia, Alberto (2017), *La crítica de la crítica: Inconsecuentes, insustanciales, impotentes, prepotentes y equidistantes*, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-8432318658](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-8432318658 "Special:BookSources/978-8432318658")
64. **[^](#cite_ref-487)** Since [Beevor (2006)](#CITEREFBeevor2006) p. 82 suggests 7,000 members of some 115,000 clergy were killed, the proportion could well be lower.
